content.text: Hallucination means the model produces
plausible but incorrect or ungrounded

content—fabricated citations, wrong API parameters, or confident false claims. It is not a random bug but a consequence of next-token prediction trained to sound fluent. Agent systems must treat LLM output as untrusted until verified by tools, retrieval, or tests.
What it is
The model assigns high probability to tokens that fit the prompt pattern, not to tokens that are factually true. Without grounding, it may invent function names, database columns, or policy details.
Intrinsic
hallucination contradicts known training facts;
extrinsic
hallucination adds details not present in provided context (e.g., RAG passages).
Why it matters

Autonomous agents act on generated plans. A hallucinated tool name fails at runtime; a hallucinated "success" message may skip error handling. Trust erosion and compliance risk increase when users cannot distinguish grounded answers from fluent guesses.
How it works
Mechanisms
Pattern completion: legal or medical prompts elicit authoritative-sounding but false specifics
Exposure bias: training on gold text; at inference the model sees its own errors
Overconfidence: softmax peaks on wrong tokens when context is ambiguous
Long outputs: error rate compounds over many tokens in agent traces
Benefits of understanding hallucination
Informs architecture: retrieval, tools, and human approval become requirements, not extras
Guides eval design: measure factual accuracy on agent tasks, not fluency alone
Clarifies when smaller or reasoning-tuned models help versus hurt
Trade-offs
Aggressive refusal tuning reduces false claims but may increase false "I don't know" responses
RAG reduces extrinsic hallucination but can cause confabulation merging unrelated chunks
Lower temperature reduces variance but does not eliminate factual errors
Verification loops add latency and cost
Mitigation strategies
Ground with retrieval (RAG) and cite sources; require quotes for claims
Force tool use for facts: weather, prices, database lookups via APIs
Structured outputs with schema validation and automatic retry on parse failure
Self-check prompts or critic models—second pass flags unsupported claims
Human-in-the-loop for high-stakes actions (payments, deletes, legal)
Agentic AI connection
Reliable agents follow
verify-then-act

: generate plan → call read-only tools to confirm assumptions → execute mutating tools only after checks. Logprob thresholds can trigger "verify with search" branches when confidence is low. Never chain mutating tools on unverified LLM assertions alone.
Common mistakes
Trusting natural-language "I checked the database" without a tool trace
Evaluating agents on single-turn QA instead of multi-step action accuracy
Assuming RAG eliminates hallucination without chunk relevance checks
Showing users confident UI for unverified model statements
Best practices
Register only allowed tools; reject unknown function names at the API layer
Return tool results as messages so the model re-grounds each turn
Track hallucination rate on domain-specific eval sets
Prefer "unknown" training and prompts over fabricated specifics
Key takeaways
Hallucination is fluent wrong content, inherent to probabilistic generation
Agents need tools, retrieval, and validation—not prompt tricks alone
Errors compound across long agent traces
Verify-then-act and schema checks are core agent design patterns
